title: The caretaker part that forgot to include yourself
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The caretaker part that forgot to include yourself

I remember the exact moment I realized something was wrong. I was standing in my kitchen, holding a plate of food I had just reheated for the third time that evening. My partner was working late, my friend was going through a breakup, my sister needed childcare, and my boss had emailed asking if I could cover an extra shift. I had said yes to every single one of them. And I hadn't eaten. Not once. Not even a bite.

Here's the thing. That plate in my hands wasn't for me. It was for my partner, who wouldn't be home for hours. I had made it, reheated it, and was now standing there like a ghost in my own life, serving everyone else while my own body went unfed. I felt a wave of something I couldn't name. Not anger. Not sadness. Something colder. Emptier. Like I had been erased.

That's the caretaker part. The one that learned early that your needs don't matter. The one that keeps you running on fumes while you pour yourself out for everyone else. And the cruelest part? It genuinely believes it's protecting you.

Let me be clear. This part isn't evil. It isn't malicious. It's a survival strategy that worked once, maybe many times, when you were small and helpless and your safety depended on keeping the people around you happy. But now it's running on autopilot, and it has forgotten one critical thing. You.

The origin story of the caretaker part

Stay with me here. I want you to think back. Not to a specific memory, necessarily, but to the feeling of being a child who needed something. Maybe you needed comfort after a nightmare. Maybe you needed help with homework. Maybe you just needed someone to notice you were tired, hungry, or scared.

What happened when you asked? Did someone show up? Or did you learn that your needs were a burden? That asking meant risking rejection, anger, or worse, being ignored entirely?

For many of us, the caretaker part formed in homes where the adults were overwhelmed, absent, or themselves wounded. You learned that the safest way to exist was to become invisible. To anticipate everyone else's needs before they even knew they had them. To become so good at taking care of others that maybe, just maybe, someone would finally take care of you.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how these patterns get locked into the body. It's not just a thought pattern. It's a physical response. Your shoulders tense when someone asks for help because your body remembers that saying no once led to punishment. Your stomach knots when you try to rest because your system associates stillness with danger. The caretaker part keeps you moving because stopping means feeling. And feeling means facing the grief of all the times no one showed up for you.

Think about it. That part of you that says "I'll handle it" when you're already exhausted. That voice that whispers "they need you more" when you're running on empty. That automatic yes that comes out before you've even considered what you actually want. That's not kindness. That's survival. And survival doesn't care if you're happy. It only cares if you're safe.

But here's the problem. You're not safe. You're depleted. And the caretaker part doesn't know the difference anymore.

The price of being the one who always gives

I want to be honest with you about what this costs. Because the wellness industry loves to talk about "self-care" as if it's a bubble bath and a green smoothie. But the real cost of the caretaker part is much deeper. It's the slow erosion of your sense of self.

When you spend years, decades, orienting toward everyone else's needs, you lose the ability to know what you need. Your own desires become foreign. Your own boundaries feel like betrayal. You start to believe that your value is entirely contingent on what you can do for others. And that is a trap that will never, ever be filled.

I worked with a therapist once who asked me a simple question. "What do you want?" I couldn't answer. Not because I was being difficult. But because I genuinely didn't know. I had been so focused on what everyone else wanted from me that my own wants had atrophied. They were like a muscle I had never used.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma and dissociation, describes this as a split in the system. You have parts that take care of others and parts that take care of the caretaker. But the caretaker part often dominates so completely that the part that needs care gets pushed into the shadows. It becomes a child that nobody sees, waiting in a room that nobody enters.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It names the invisible experience of growing up without emotional attunement, and it helped me see that my caretaker part wasn't broken. It was just doing a job that no child should have to do.

The physical toll is real too. Chronic fatigue, digestive issues, headaches, a constant low-grade inflammation in the body. Your nervous system stays in a state of sympathetic activation, always ready to respond to someone else's crisis. Deb Dana, who writes about Polyvagal Theory, calls this "mobilization without recovery." You're always moving, always giving, always available. But you never come back to rest. You never come back to yourself.

Wild, right? The part that's trying to keep you safe is actually keeping you sick.

How to start including yourself

I'm not going to give you a five-step plan to fix this overnight. Because that's not how parts work. The caretaker part didn't form in a week, and it won't dissolve in one either. But I can tell you what has helped me, slowly, painfully, and with a lot of backsliding.

First, you have to recognize the part when it shows up. Not judge it. Not try to silence it. Just notice it. "Oh, there's that part of me that's already saying yes before I've even thought about it." "There's that part that feels guilty for resting." "There's that part that believes I'm selfish if I say no."

Notice what just happened in your body. Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach drop? Did your shoulders move toward your ears? The caretaker part lives in the body, not just the mind. And when you can feel it physically, you can start to relate to it differently.

Second, you have to start practicing small no's. Not the big, dramatic boundaries that feel impossible. Tiny ones. "I can't talk right now, but I'll call you tomorrow." "No, I don't want another slice of cake." "No, I need five minutes before I respond to that email." These small no's are like reps at the gym. They build the muscle of self-inclusion. And every time you say a small no, you're telling the caretaker part that you exist too.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). It breaks down the internal resistance we feel when we try to set limits, and it helped me understand that my guilt wasn't a sign I was doing something wrong. It was a sign I was doing something new.

Third, you have to grieve. This is the part nobody wants to talk about. The caretaker part carries a lot of grief. Grief for the childhood you didn't get. Grief for the relationships where you gave and gave and never received. Grief for the parts of yourself you abandoned along the way. That grief needs space. It needs to be felt, not fixed.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work helped me see that I could hold my caretaker part with compassion instead of trying to get rid of it. That part wasn't my enemy. It was just a very tired, very young part of me that had been working overtime for decades.

Fourth, you have to experiment with receiving. This might be the hardest one. Let someone bring you a cup of tea. Let someone ask how you're doing and actually tell them. Let someone take care of you without immediately needing to return the favor. It will feel wrong at first. It will feel dangerous. But that's just the caretaker part sounding the alarm. Stay with it. Breathe through it. Let yourself be held, even for a moment.

The ongoing negotiation with your caretaker part

I'm not going to tell you that I've healed this part completely. I haven't. Just last week, I caught myself agreeing to drive a friend to an appointment when I was already running on three hours of sleep and a headache that wouldn't quit. The caretaker part was right there, offering its familiar script. "You can handle it. They need you. What kind of person would you be if you said no?"

But here's what's different now. I noticed it. I felt the pull in my chest, the familiar tightness in my throat. And instead of automatically saying yes, I paused. I took a breath. And I said, "Let me check my schedule and get back to you." That pause was everything. It was a crack of light in a door that had been sealed shut for years.

I did end up saying yes that time. But I said yes from a place of choice, not compulsion. And I also made sure I ate something before I left. I drank water. I told myself, "You matter too. Even when you're helping someone else, you matter too."

That's the work. It's not about killing the caretaker part. It's about expanding the system so that there's room for you. Room for your needs. Room for your wants. Room for the part of you that has been waiting, patiently, desperately, for someone to finally notice that you're here too.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, says that every part has a positive intention. The caretaker part's intention is to keep you safe and connected. It just doesn't realize that safety now requires including yourself. It doesn't know that connection with others, real connection, can only happen when you're also connected to yourself.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). It helped me see my caretaker part not as a problem to be solved, but as a protector that needed to be thanked and gently updated.

So here's what I want you to take from this. Not a prescription. Not a checklist. Just this. Your caretaker part is not wrong. It's just tired. And it needs to hear, from you, that you're okay. That you can handle things now. That it can rest. That you will include yourself in the circle of care.

Start small. Notice the part. Thank it for its service. And then, very gently, ask it to make a little room. For you.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect specifically
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work
